what: Search for the required sample size
title: Getting started with anovapowersim
text: Use power_n() search sample size needed reach requested power. example mixed design one two-level -subject factor (cond) one four-level within-subject factor (stim). tests cond:stim interaction 90% power detect partial eta squared 0.14. power_n() reports required number subjects per -subject cell corresponding total N. purely within-subject design, reported n_per_cell total sample size. output table uses compact column names: num_df den_df ANOVA degrees freedom, ncp noncentrality parameter, power_calc calculated noncentral-F power, power_sim simulation estimate. example uses 1000 simulations speed. Use least 5000 simulations stable estimate; package default 10000.
code: power_n(   between = c(cond = 2), # cond has 2 levels   within = c(stim = 4), # stim has 4 levels   term = "cond:stim",   target_pes = 0.14,   alpha = 0.05,   power = 0.90,   n_sims = 1000, # use 5000+ for a more precise estimate   seed = 123 # for reproducibility ) #> <anovapowersim_curve> #>   term:          'cond:stim' #>   target power:  0.900 #>   alpha:         0.05 #>   effect size:   pes = 0.14 #>   n values:      6 per-cell sample sizes visited #>   sims per cell size: 1000 #>   SS type:       III #>   n needed for between-subjects cell: 17 #>   total N needed: 34 #>  #>  n_per_cell total_n n_sims num_df den_df    ncp power_calc power_sim #>          13      26   1000      3     72 11.721      0.808     0.795 #>          16      32   1000      3     90 14.651      0.897     0.885 #>          17      34   1000      3     96 15.628      0.917     0.912 #>          18      36   1000      3    102 16.605      0.934     0.936 #>          20      40   1000      3    114 18.558      0.958     0.946 #>          26      52   1000      3    150 24.419      0.991     0.991

title: Getting started with anovapowersim
text: Use power_curve() estimate power across explicitly chosen sample sizes. result tidy table can passed plot_power_curve().
code: pc <- power_curve(   between = c(cond = 2),   within = c(stim = 2),   term = "cond:stim",   target_pes = 0.14,   n_range = c(16, 20, 23, 28),   n_sims = 1000,   seed = 123 ) pc #> <anovapowersim_curve> #>   term:          'cond:stim' #>   target power:  <not specified> #>   alpha:         0.05 #>   effect size:   pes = 0.14 #>   n values:      4 per-cell sample sizes visited #>   sims per cell size: 1000 #>   SS type:       III #>   n needed for between-subjects cell: <not reached> #>   total N needed: <not reached> #>  #>  n_per_cell total_n n_sims num_df den_df   ncp power_calc power_sim #>          16      32   1000      1     30 4.884      0.571     0.557 #>          20      40   1000      1     38 6.186      0.679     0.666 #>          23      46   1000      1     44 7.163      0.745     0.735 #>          28      56   1000      1     54 8.791      0.829     0.831 plot_power_curve(   pc,   power_lines = c(0.80, 0.90) )

title: Getting started with anovapowersim
text: larger simulation runs, set parallel = TRUE. cores omitted, anovapowersim uses one fewer available cores reports number selected. Set cores explicitly fixed number workers required.
code: power_n(   between = c(cond = 2),   within = c(stim = 2),   term = "cond:stim",   target_pes = 0.14,   power = 0.90,   n_sims = 5000,   parallel = TRUE,   cores = 4,   seed = 123 )

title: Covariance and nonsphericity
text: Cell names combine within-factor levels underscores. within = c(time = 3, condition = 2), expected names time1_condition1, time1_condition2, time2_condition1, time2_condition2, time3_condition1, time3_condition2. Correlation names join pair cells :. Every measurement uses common sd; unlisted pairs use default_correlation. Pair-specific correlations can produce nonsphericity even though diagonal variances equal. package warns whenever defaults actually needed: Omitting covariance balanced simulation uses sd = 1 correlation 0.5 every within-subject pair. Calling within_covariance() without sd uses common sd = 1. correlations name every pair, warning reports many pairs undefined. default_correlation fills undefined pairs; correlations explicitly listed user never replaced. raw covariance matrix defines variances correlations directly use defaults. diagonal variances must equal.
code: covariance <- within_covariance(   sd = 1,   default_correlation = 0.5,   correlations = c(     "time1_condition1:time1_condition2" = 0.6,     "time2_condition1:time2_condition2" = 0.7   ) )

title: Covariance and nonsphericity
text: Pass object balanced simulation function: custom covariance structure calibrates target effect generates every simulated dataset. package projects covariance tested term’s contrast space calculates population Greenhouse–Geisser epsilon. power_calc applies epsilon degrees freedom noncentrality. population epsilon 1, power_sim uses simulated dataset’s sample-estimated Greenhouse–Geisser-adjusted p-value sums-- squares type II III. Type tests remain uncorrected issue warning. Huynh–Feldt corrections currently implemented.
code: power_n(   within = c(time = 3, condition = 2),   term = "time:condition",   target_pes = 0.14,   power = 0.90,   n_sims = 5000,   covariance = covariance,   seed = 123 )

what: Why the relative means pattern matters
title: Covariance and nonsphericity
text: within-subject term one degree freedom, effect vector, theta, multidimensional contrast space. covariance space Psi. sphericity, Psi rotationally symmetric, rotating fixed-length theta change omnibus power. nonsphericity, components unequal weights: power depends theta aligns eigenspaces Psi. answers initially surprising question: target_pes covariance identical, can power differ? conventional noncentral-F calculation uses scalar aggregate signal magnitude related ||theta||^2 / tr(Psi). therefore reports power_calc every fixed-length direction. actual univariate repeated-measures statistic weighted noncentral components nonsphericity, power_sim conditional relative mean direction. Greenhouse–Geisser correction changes reference degrees freedom rejection threshold; remove underlying direction sensitivity. uncorrected type-path can also direction-sensitive. Use means_pattern() state relative shape. Sparse cells listed raw value zero. Factors outside tested term broadcast, raw values projected exactly onto requested term one positive scale factor calibrates target_pes. index time = 4 generated level name time = "time4" equivalent. Multiplying every raw value positive constant gives calibrated means. Reversing signs gives omnibus power, adding intercept lower-order effect change projected target-term component. Projection may make final target-term means nonzero cells omitted sparse input. following validation audit used four-level AR(1) covariance, target_pes = 0.20, population epsilon 0.7244823, n = 25, alpha = 0.05 patterns: observed corrected simulation powers power_sim = 0.945 power_sim = 0.896, respectively, power_calc identical. gap motivation means_pattern; materially larger Monte Carlo instability simulation count.
code: curve_pattern <- means_pattern(   time = 1, value =  1,   time = 2, value = -1,   time = 3, value = -1,   time = 4, value =  1 )  level_pattern <- means_pattern(time = "time4", value = 1) ar_covariance <- within_covariance(   sd = 1,   correlations = c(     "time1:time2" = 0.800,     "time1:time3" = 0.640,     "time1:time4" = 0.512,     "time2:time3" = 0.800,     "time2:time4" = 0.640,     "time3:time4" = 0.800   ) )  power_achieved(   within = c(time = 4), term = "time", target_pes = 0.20, n = 25,   covariance = ar_covariance, means_pattern = curve_pattern,   n_sims = 10000, seed = 55 )  power_achieved(   within = c(time = 4), term = "time", target_pes = 0.20, n = 25,   covariance = ar_covariance, means_pattern = level_pattern,   n_sims = 10000, seed = 55 )

title: Power for unbalanced designs
text: Define every cell cell_design(). Factor values identify cell, n number subjects -subject group, m population mean. Name repeated-measures factors within; remaining factors -subject factors. common population SD specified separately unbalanced_covariance(). repeated-measures designs, n must appear every within-subject row belonging given -subject group. means define complete effect pattern, including main effects interactions. single marginal SD shared across groups within-subject cells, preventing unequal cell sizes combined unequal variances classical ANOVA test. Every factor must least two observed levels, every factor-level combination must defined exactly . cells missing, cell_design() reports ones. Supply default_n default_m together fill missing cells automatically:
code: unbalanced_design <- cell_design(   group = "control",   time = "pre",  n = 22, m = 10.0,   group = "control",   time = "post", n = 22, m = 11.0,   group = "treatment", time = "pre",  n = 31, m = 10.1,   group = "treatment", time = "post", n = 31, m = 12.4,   within = "time" ) cell_design(   group = "control",   time = "pre",  n = 22, m = 10.0,   group = "control",   time = "post", n = 22, m = 11.0,   group = "treatment", time = "pre",  n = 31, m = 10.1,   within = "time",   default_n = 31, default_m = 12.4 )

title: Power for unbalanced designs
text: Use unbalanced_covariance() define common SD , repeated measures, correlation structure. power_unbalanced() constructs one covariance matrix uses every -subject group. covariance omitted, power_unbalanced() warns using common sd = 1 , repeated measures, correlation 0.5 every pair. Calling unbalanced_covariance() without sd likewise warns sd = 1 used. correlations named, separate warning reports number undefined pairs: default_correlation applies undefined pairs alter explicitly supplied correlations. purely -subject design, correlations apply warning mentions common SD. one within-subject factor, pair names use levels, "pre:post".
code: power_unbalanced(   design = unbalanced_design,   term = "group:time",   covariance = unbalanced_covariance(     sd = 2,     default_correlation = 0.5,     correlations = c("pre:post" = 0.7)   ),   n_sims = 5000,   parallel = TRUE,   seed = 123 )

title: Power for unbalanced designs

text: anovapowersim designed simple easy use first, means limitations now. support: Covariates (ANCOVAs) Sample-size searches power curves unbalanced designs Huynh-Feldt corrections power simulations. Greenhouse-Geisser-corrected simulated tests supported sums--squares type II III custom covariance implies epsilon < 1; type tests remain uncorrected. Heteroskedastic ANOVA. Simulation functions require one common marginal variance; unequal correlations Greenhouse–Geisser corrections remain supported repeated-measures designs. Simulation functions warn default common sd = 1 default within-subject correlation 0.5 used. Covariance specifications also warn correlations defined; default correlation fills undefined pairs. - Simple main effects/pairwise comparisons

title: Compute the mean-deviation scaling factor from a change in partial eta squared — compute_scale_factor
text: single positive numeric value k. k > 1 amplifies effect, k < 1 shrinks , k == 1 leaves unchanged.

title: Compute the mean-deviation scaling factor from a change in partial eta squared — compute_scale_factor
text: derivation straightforward: partial eta squared can written pes = SS_effect / (SS_effect + C), C part denominator held fixed package's rescaling. Thus pes / (1 - pes) scales target effect's sum squares. Scaling term's deviations k scales target effect's sum squares k^2, required multiplier $$k = \sqrt{\frac{p_{\mathrm{new}} / (1 - p_{\mathrm{new}})}                       {p_{\mathrm{old}} / (1 - p_{\mathrm{old}})}}.$$

text: Calculation-search per--cell sample size needed reach requested power balanced factorial ANOVA design. Unlike power_n(), function run simulations, fit ANOVA models, call car; numerator degrees freedom, denominator degrees freedom, noncentrality, calculated power obtained directly balanced design.

title: Calculate the sample size needed for target ANOVA power — power_n_calc
code: power_n_calc(   between = NULL,   within = NULL,   term,   target_pes,   power = 0.9,   alpha = 0.05,   n_start = NULL,   n_max = 5000,   gpower = FALSE,   epsilon = 1 )

title: Calculate the sample size needed for target ANOVA power — power_n_calc
text: Named integer vector -subject factor level counts, e.g. c(group = 2). Use NULL -subject factors. within Named integer vector within-subject factor level counts, e.g. c(time = 3, condition = 4). Use NULL within-subject factors. term Character scalar naming ANOVA term test, e.g. "group:time". Interaction terms order-insensitive; "time:group" resolves "group:time" design's factor order. target_pes Target partial eta squared term. power Desired target power. alpha Significance threshold. n_start Starting sample size per -subject cell. NULL, starts smallest value valid calculated-power degrees freedom. n_max Maximum sample size per -subject cell. gpower Logical; TRUE, use GPower-style noncentrality convention lambda = total_n * f^2. default FALSE uses lambda = den_df * f^2. GPower's estimates can differ target_pes, especially small samples terms degrees freedom; warning issued gpower = TRUE. default gpower = FALSE recommended. epsilon Population nonsphericity correction within-subject component term. Must lie theoretical lower bound 1 / within_term_df 1. default 1 assumes sphericity. Values 1 multiply numerator degrees freedom, denominator degrees freedom, noncentrality parameter. Nonsphericity corrections apply purely -subject terms.

what: Estimate ANOVA effect-size sensitivity at a fixed sample size — power_sensitivity
title: Estimate ANOVA effect-size sensitivity at a fixed sample size — power_sensitivity
text: Searches minimum detectable partial eta squared fixed sample size target power. Calculated power supplies efficient initial estimate. Explicit simulations bracket target refine effect-size bracket using interpolation midpoint fallback.

title: Estimate ANOVA effect-size sensitivity at a fixed sample size — power_sensitivity
code: power_sensitivity(   between = NULL,   within = NULL,   term,   n,   power = 0.9,   n_sims = 10000,   alpha = 0.05,   ss_type = "III",   pes_min = 1e-06,   pes_max = 0.99,   pes_tol = 0.001,   gpower = FALSE,   progress = interactive(),   parallel = FALSE,   cores = NULL,   seed = NULL,   covariance = NULL,   means_pattern = NULL )

title: Estimate ANOVA effect-size sensitivity at a fixed sample size — power_sensitivity
text: Named integer vector -subject factor level counts, e.g. c(group = 2). Use NULL -subject factors. within Named integer vector within-subject factor level counts, e.g. c(time = 3, condition = 4). Use NULL within-subject factors. term Character scalar naming ANOVA term test, e.g. "group:time". Interaction terms order-insensitive; "time:group" resolves "group:time" design's factor order. n Number subjects per -subject cell. purely within-subject design, total sample size. power Desired target power. n_sims Number simulated datasets per sample size. alpha Significance threshold. ss_type Sums--squares type tested ANOVA term. "III" default order-invariant tests unbalanced designs. Use "" reproduce sequential stats::aov() tests. Greenhouse–Geisser-corrected simulated p-values (see covariance) available "III" "II"; "", simulated p-values always use uncorrected univariate test, warning issued supplied covariance yields population epsilon 1. pes_min Lower bound partial eta-squared search interval. pes_max Upper bound partial eta-squared search interval. pes_tol Maximum width final simulated partial eta-squared bracket. gpower Logical; TRUE, calibrate means GPower-style noncentrality convention lambda = total_n * f^2. default FALSE calibrates empirical reference dataset target_pes, equivalent lambda = den_df * f^2 fitted ANOVA. GPower's estimates can differ target_pes, especially small samples terms degrees freedom; warning issued gpower = TRUE. default gpower = FALSE recommended. progress Logical; TRUE, show text progress bar. parallel Logical; TRUE, run simulations sample size via future ecosystem. cores Optional positive integer number cores use parallel = TRUE. NULL, uses one fewer number available cores, minimum one. seed Optional integer seed reproducibility. covariance Optional within-subject covariance specification within_covariance() numeric covariance matrix. default NULL uses standard deviations 1 compound-symmetric correlation 0.5 issues warning stating defaults. within_covariance() specification issues warning correlation pairs omitted: default_correlation applies undefined pairs, explicitly defined correlations unchanged. measurements must one common marginal variance. supplied matrix must therefore equal diagonal entries one row column per within-subject cell; named matrices reordered design's cell order. Unequal correlations remain supported. terms containing within-subject factors, matrix also used derive term-specific population Greenhouse–Geisser epsilon power_calc. population epsilon 1, power_sim also based simulated dataset's Greenhouse–Geisser-corrected p-value (car::Anova()) rather uncorrected univariate test, power_sim power_calc estimate corrected test. means_pattern Optional relative cell-mean shape created means_pattern(). sparse values projected onto term, normalized, uniformly rescaled reach target_pes. NULL, simulations use package's deterministic linear/Kronecker pattern. multi-df nonspherical within-subject terms, simulated power conditional direction, explicit pattern recommended expected shape known.

title: Specify a within-subject covariance structure — within_covariance
text: Creates readable covariance specification power_n() power_curve(). Supply one common standard deviation default correlation, override individual correlation pairs differ. Measurement names generated within design; example, within = c(time = 2, condition = 2) creates time1_condition1, time1_condition2, time2_condition1, time2_condition2.

title: Specify a within-subject covariance structure — within_covariance
code: within_covariance(sd = 1, default_correlation = 0.5, correlations = NULL)

title: Specify a within-subject covariance structure — within_covariance
text: sd Common positive finite standard deviation every measurement. omitted, 1 used warning issued. default_correlation Finite correlation (-1, 1) used pairs listed correlations. specification resolved design, warning identifies many pairs defined makes clear default applies pairs. correlations Optional named numeric vector pair-specific correlations. Name pair "cell1:cell2", example "time1:time2" = 0.7. Pair order matter.

text: anovapowersim_covariance_spec object. power_n() power_curve() resolve within design construct covariance matrix sd^2 * R, R correlation matrix. also derive tested term's population Greenhouse–Geisser epsilon resolved matrix apply power_calc.

title: anovapowersim (development version)
text: Added means_pattern() optional means_pattern argument power_curve(), power_n(), power_achieved(), power_sensitivity(), design_term_means(). Sparse relative cell values accept one-based indices exact generated balanced-design level names, broadcast omitted factors, projected onto requested ANOVA term uniform calibration target_pes. Balanced simulations now use normalized centered-linear/Kronecker direction explicit pattern supplied. Results record print whether documented default custom pattern used. Balanced simulations now warn implicit default direction consequential: tested within-subject component one degree freedom population Greenhouse–Geisser epsilon one. nonsphericity, power_sim can depend mean direction even target_pes covariance fixed. Calculation-functions retain conventional direction-insensitive noncentral-F approximation warn; power_unbalanced() already receives literal means. gpower = TRUE warning now issued whenever gpower = TRUE used, within-subject terms one degree freedom. G*Power’s estimates can differ target_pes broadly ; default gpower = FALSE remains recommended. power_n() now rejects n_start values n_max instead running first simulation outside requested search range. Balanced simulation results now retain full-precision simulated power used adaptive searches report valid/failed fit counts. Printed power values remain formatted three decimals. Breaking: simulation APIs now require one common marginal variance. within_covariance() replaces default_sd sd removes measurement-specific standard_deviations; direct covariance matrices must equal diagonal variances. unbalanced designs, remove cell-level sd default_sd cell_design() supply common SD unbalanced_covariance(sd = ...). Unequal correlations Greenhouse–Geisser correction remain supported. Simulation functions now warn omitted covariance causes common sd = 1 within-subject correlation 0.5 defaults used. covariance constructors warn sd omitted, resolved covariance specifications warn default_correlation fills unnamed pairs preserving every explicitly supplied correlation. Added experimental, development-version-cell_design(), unbalanced_covariance(), power_unbalanced() functions simulation-power analysis fixed unbalanced allocation user-defined cell means sample sizes common standard deviation optional within-subject correlations. Results include simulated power partial eta-squared diagnostics, deliberately omit calculated power. power_unbalanced() derives population Greenhouse–Geisser epsilon covariance matrix shared across -subject cells, reports $epsilon, bases power_sim Greenhouse–Geisser-corrected simulated p-value whenever epsilon 1 (requires ss_type "III" "II"; warning issued ss_type = "" combined non-spherical design). Breaking (experimental): cell_design() now takes within argument (character vector within-subject factor names, NULL) stores returned design; power_unbalanced() longer accepts within reads design instead. Move within = ... power_unbalanced() cell_design(). cell_design() gained default_n default_m. Supply auto-fill missing cells complete factorial design; supplying one error, supplying neither requires every cell defined explicitly (). cell_design() now reports exact missing factor-level combinations design incomplete, instead count, errors clearly factor fewer two observed levels (previously surfaced later, inside power_unbalanced(), unhelpful low-level contrast-fitting error). within-subject n-consistency check (n identical across within-subject rows -subject cell) now runs cell_design() construction time; previously surfaced inside power_unbalanced(). Added experimental, development-version-power_achieved() function simulation-based achieved-power estimation fixed sample size partial eta squared. Added experimental, development-version-power_sensitivity() function simulation-based minimum-detectable partial eta-squared searches fixed sample size target power. Added experimental, development-version-power_achieved_calc() power_sensitivity_calc() functions equivalent fixed-sample analyses using calculated noncentral-F power without simulations. Added power_n_calc() calculated-power, simulation-free sample-size searches balanced ANOVA designs. Added epsilon argument power_n_calc() calculated-power nonsphericity corrections terms containing within-subject factors. Added within_covariance() covariance argument power_n() power_curve() simulations can use custom common SD within-subject correlation structure. functions now derive term-specific population Greenhouse–Geisser epsilon covariance apply calculated power. power_curve(), power_n(), power_achieved(), power_sensitivity(), power_n_calc(), power_achieved_calc(), power_sensitivity_calc(), design_term_means() now warn gpower = TRUE combined term whose within-subject component one degree freedom (.e. within factor two levels). case target_pes gpower = TRUE equal partial eta squared actually achieved – mirrors property GPower’s “Cohen (1988)” repeated-measures convention, adjust number measurements, rather bug package (gpower = TRUE remains exact replica GPower’s noncentrality formula). Use default gpower = FALSE target_pes match reported expected partial eta squared exactly. supplied covariance yields population Greenhouse–Geisser epsilon 1, power_curve(), power_n(), power_achieved(), power_sensitivity() now base power_sim simulated dataset’s Greenhouse–Geisser-corrected p-value instead uncorrected univariate test, power_sim power_calc estimate corrected test rather diverging non-sphericity. correction requires ss_type "III" "II"; "", simulated p-values remain uncorrected, functions now warn ss_type = "" combined covariance whose derived epsilon 1.
